modules.title: IPC Validity & Privileges
modules.summary: When does your IPC actually expire? What counts for single-pilot recency? The dates and gotchas that trip people up.

content: CASR 61.880 — IPC is valid until the end of the 12th month after the month in which the holder passes the flight test.
CASR 61.880 — If an IPC is completed within 3 months before the existing IPC expires, the new IPC runs from the existing expiry, not the new check date.
CASR 61.855 — An instrument rating authorises a pilot to fly under the IFR, or at night under the VFR.
CASR 61.875 — Single-pilot IFR currency: must have completed at least 1 hour of IFR flight (including 1 instrument approach) in a single-pilot operation within the previous 6 months.
12 months — IPC validity period (to end of 12th month after month of passing)
3 months — early renewal window in which new IPC extends from old expiry, not new check date
6 months — single-pilot IFR recent experience currency period (1hr IFR incl. 1 approach)
Counting 12 months from the test date — IPC validity runs to the END of the 12th month after the month of passing, not 12 months from the exact date.
Renewing early without understanding the extension rule — completing a new IPC within 3 months before expiry means the new IPC runs from the OLD expiry date, not the new check date.
Assuming a current IPC means single-pilot IFR is current — single-pilot IFR requires a separate 6-month recency check: 1hr IFR including 1 approach in a single-pilot operation.

content.question: How long is an IPC valid from the date the holder passes the flight test?
content.answer: Until the end of the 12th month after the month in which the holder passes the flight test.
distractors: For 12 months from the date on which the holder passes the flight test.
Until the end of the 6th month after the month in which the holder passes the flight test.
Until the end of the 24th month after the month in which the holder passes the flight test.

content.question: If you complete an IPC within 3 months before your existing IPC expires, when does the new IPC expire?
content.answer: The end of the 12th month after the validity of the existing check — not after the new check date.
distractors: Until the end of the 12th month after the date the new check was completed.
Until the end of the 6th month after the validity of the existing check.
For 12 months from the date of the new check, regardless of when the old one expires.

content.question: Under CASR 61.855, what does an instrument rating authorise a pilot to do?
content.answer: To pilot an aircraft under the IFR, or at night under the VFR.
distractors: To pilot an aircraft under the IFR only.
To pilot an aircraft under the IFR, at night under the VFR, and in IMC under the VFR.
To pilot an aircraft under the IFR and to conduct instrument approaches in VMC.

content.question: Under what conditions may a holder of an instrument rating conduct a circling approach under the IFR?
content.answer: If their most recent IPC included a circling approach; or they passed the IR flight test within the previous 12 months and that test included a circling approach; or they are successfully participating in an operator's T&C system that includes circling approaches.
distractors: Provided they hold a current IPC — no separate circling approach currency is required.
If their most recent IPC included a circling approach within the previous 6 months.
If they have conducted at least one circling approach within the previous 90 days.

content.question: What must a pilot do before conducting a single-pilot IFR operation under CASR 61.875?
content.answer: Conduct a flight or simulated flight under the IFR in a single-pilot operation within the previous 6 months, of at least 1 hour duration, including at least one instrument approach or simulated instrument approach.
distractors: Conduct a flight or simulated flight under the IFR in a single-pilot operation within the previous 90 days, of at least 30 minutes duration, including at least one instrument approach.
Conduct a flight or simulated flight under the IFR in a single-pilot operation within the previous 12 months, of at least 1 hour duration, including at least one instrument approach.
Conduct at least 3 instrument approaches in a single-pilot aircraft within the previous 6 months.

content.question: Can the IPC recent experience requirements be considered met by an operator proficiency check?
content.answer: Yes — if the holder has successfully completed an operator proficiency check covering IFR operations within the previous 3 months, or is successfully participating in an operator's training and checking system for IFR operations.
distractors: No — only flight time under the IFR counts toward recent experience requirements.
Yes — if the holder has completed an operator proficiency check within the previous 6 months.
Yes — if the holder has completed an operator proficiency check within the previous 90 days.

modules.title: Recent Experience Requirements
modules.summary: How many approaches do you need in 90 days? Does a 2D approach count toward 3D currency? The recency rules that catch people out.

content: CASR 61.870 — To fly under the IFR, a pilot must have conducted at least 3 instrument approach operations within the previous 90 days.
CASR 61.870 — To fly a particular aircraft category under the IFR, at least 1 approach in an aircraft of the same category within 90 days is required.
CASR 61.870 — Separate 90-day currency is required for 2D approaches, 3D approaches, azimuth guidance approaches, and CDI approaches.
3 approaches — minimum instrument approaches required within 90 days to remain IFR current
90 days — recency window for all IFR instrument approach currency requirements
1 approach — minimum same-category approach within 90 days for that aircraft category
Counting total approaches without checking sub-type currency — 3 total approaches are needed, but 2D, 3D, azimuth, and CDI approach types each also require at least 1 within 90 days separately.
Using a different aircraft category to maintain recency — 1 same-category approach is required; helicopter approaches do not count for aeroplane currency.
Assuming IPC renewal resets the 90-day approach recency — an IPC does not automatically satisfy the 90-day approach count requirement.

content.question: What recent experience is required to fly under the IFR?
content.answer: At least 3 instrument approach operations within the previous 90 days.
distractors: At least 1 instrument approach operation within the previous 90 days.
At least 2 instrument approach operations within the previous 90 days.
At least 3 instrument approach operations within the previous 6 months.

content.question: What recent experience is required to pilot an aircraft of a particular category under the IFR?
content.answer: At least one instrument approach in an aircraft of the same category within the previous 90 days.
distractors: At least 3 instrument approaches in an aircraft of the same category within the previous 90 days.
At least one instrument approach in an aircraft of the same category within the previous 6 months.
At least one instrument approach in any aircraft within the previous 90 days.

content.question: What recent experience is required to conduct a 3D instrument approach?
content.answer: At least one 3D instrument approach within the previous 90 days.
distractors: At least one 3D instrument approach within the previous 6 months.
At least two 3D instrument approaches within the previous 90 days.
At least one 2D instrument approach within the previous 90 days.

content.question: What recent experience is required to conduct a 2D instrument approach?
content.answer: At least one 2D instrument approach within the previous 90 days.
distractors: At least one 2D instrument approach within the previous 6 months.
At least two 2D instrument approaches within the previous 90 days.
At least one 3D instrument approach within the previous 90 days.

content.question: What recent experience is required to conduct an approach using azimuth guidance?
content.answer: At least one 2D instrument approach using azimuth guidance within the previous 90 days.
distractors: At least one instrument approach using azimuth guidance within the previous 6 months.
At least two 2D instrument approaches using azimuth guidance within the previous 90 days.
At least one 2D instrument approach within the previous 90 days — azimuth guidance does not require separate currency.

content.question: What recent experience is required to conduct an approach using a CDI?
content.answer: At least one instrument approach using a CDI within the previous 90 days.
distractors: At least one instrument approach using a CDI within the previous 6 months.
At least two instrument approaches using a CDI within the previous 90 days.
At least one instrument approach using azimuth guidance within the previous 90 days.

modules.title: Aircraft Equipment
modules.summary: What instruments must be fitted for IFR? When is an altitude alerter required? The equipment list examiners quiz you on.

content: Part 91 MOS 26.08 — IFR aeroplane equipment: approved GNSS, AI, ASI (pitot heat on at least one), altimeter (alt static source), VSI, turn-and-slip, DG, compass, OAT, and clock.
Part 91 MOS 26.25 — Altitude alerting: piston and unpressurised turbine must have it above FL150 in CTA; pressurised turbine must have it in any CTA.
Part 91 MOS 26.04 — Departure with inoperative required equipment is only permitted under an approved permissible unserviceability (PU).
FL150 — minimum altitude above which piston/unpressurised turbine must have altitude alerting in controlled airspace
1 red beacon or 2 white strobes — minimum anti-collision lighting requirement (or combination)
10 systems — minimum IFR equipment items under Part 91 MOS 26.08 (GNSS, AI, ASI, alt, alt-static, VSI, T+S, DG, compass, OAT, clock)
Forgetting pitot heat on at least one ASI — it is not enough to have an ASI; Part 91 MOS 26.08 specifically requires pitot heat on at least one.
Confusing altitude alerting thresholds — pressurised turbine needs altitude alerting in ANY CTA; piston and unpressurised turbine only above FL150.
Departing with unserviceable equipment without a valid PU — inoperative required equipment must be covered by an approved permissible unserviceability, not just noted in the maintenance release.

content.question: What instruments and equipment are required for IFR aeroplane flight under Part 91 MOS 26.08?
content.answer: Approved GNSS, attitude indicator (AI), airspeed indicator (ASI — with pitot heat on at least one), altimeter (with alternate static source), VSI, turn-and-slip (T+S), directional gyro (DG), compass, OAT, and clock (can be worn).

content.question: Under Part 91 MOS 26.25, which aircraft must be fitted with altitude alerting equipment above FL150 in controlled airspace?
content.answer: Piston-engine aircraft, and unpressurised turbine-engine aircraft operating above FL150. Pressurised turbine-engine aircraft must have altitude alerting in any controlled airspace.

content.question: What must altitude alerting equipment do under Part 91 MOS 26.25?
content.answer: Include an assigned altitude indicator; alert flight crew if the aircraft approaches a preselected altitude; and alert flight crew — including by an aural warning — if the aircraft deviates from a preselected altitude.

content.question: What are the anti-collision light requirements under Part 91 MOS 26.22?
content.answer: 1 red beacon, or 2 white strobes, or a combination of these.
distractors: 2 red beacons, or 2 white strobes, or a combination of these.
1 red beacon and 2 white strobes.
1 red beacon, or 1 white strobe, or a combination of these.

content.question: What landing light is required when operating by night?
content.answer: At least 1 landing light.

content.question: Under Part 91 MOS 26.04, can an aircraft depart IFR with inoperative required equipment?
content.answer: Only if the equipment is inoperative due to a defect approved as a permissible unserviceability, and is fitted or carried in accordance with that permissible unserviceability.

modules.title: Speed Definitions & Comms Failure
modules.summary: What's the difference between Vyse and Vsse? What do you actually do when you lose comms? Speeds and procedures worth knowing cold.

content: AIP ENR 1.5 — On comms failure in controlled airspace: squawk 7600; maintain last assigned level or MSA for 3 minutes at clearance limit; proceed on latest ATC clearance.
AIP ENR 1.5 — If being radar vectored on comms failure: maintain last assigned vector for 2 minutes, then proceed on latest ATC clearance.
AIP ENR 1.5 — If in holding on comms failure: complete one more holding pattern before proceeding on latest ATC clearance.
7600 — squawk code for communications failure
3 minutes — hold time at clearance limit (altitude or route restriction) after comms failure before proceeding
2 minutes — duration to maintain last assigned vector when being radar vectored after comms failure
1 pattern — additional complete holding pattern to fly after comms failure while in the hold
Immediately proceeding on clearance after comms failure with a route/altitude restriction — must hold for 3 minutes at the clearance limit BEFORE tracking on.
Applying the 3-minute hold rule when being radar vectored — radar vectoring comms failure requires only 2 minutes on the last vector, not 3 minutes of holding.
Forgetting to transmit blind in VMC Class G — in VMC Class G after comms failure, must broadcast intentions prefixed 'TRANSMITTING BLIND' before any action.

content.question: Define VMCA.
content.answer: Minimum Control Speed in the air — the minimum speed at which directional control can be maintained with the critical engine inoperative, live engine at take-off power, gear retracted, maximum 5° bank toward the live engine, propeller feathered if auto-feather is fitted.
distractors: Minimum Control Speed on the ground — the minimum speed whilst on the ground at which directional control can be maintained using only aerodynamic controls, with the critical engine inoperative and live engine at take-off power.
Single Engine Safety Speed — the speed below which intentional simulated engine failures should not be conducted, providing a margin above the minimum control speed.
Single Engine Best Rate of Climb Speed (blue radial) — the speed at which the best rate of climb is achieved with the critical engine inoperative, live engine at take-off power, and gear retracted.

content.question: Define VMCG.
content.answer: Minimum Control Speed on the ground — the minimum speed whilst on the ground at which directional control can be maintained using only aerodynamic controls, with the critical engine inoperative and live engine at take-off power.
distractors: Minimum Control Speed in the air — the minimum speed at which directional control can be maintained with the critical engine inoperative, live engine at take-off power, gear retracted, maximum 5° bank toward the live engine.
Single Engine Safety Speed — the speed below which intentional simulated engine failures should not be conducted.
Single Engine Best Rate of Climb Speed (blue radial) — the speed at which the best rate of climb is achieved with the critical engine inoperative, live engine at take-off power, and gear retracted.

content.question: Define VSSE.
content.answer: Single Engine Safety Speed — the speed below which intentional simulated engine failures should not be conducted. It is manufacturer-stated and provides a margin above VMCA.
distractors: Minimum Control Speed in the air — the minimum speed at which directional control can be maintained with the critical engine inoperative, live engine at take-off power, gear retracted.
Single Engine Best Rate of Climb Speed (blue radial) — the speed at which the best rate of climb is achieved with the critical engine inoperative.
Minimum Control Speed on the ground — the minimum speed on the ground at which directional control can be maintained using only aerodynamic controls.

content.question: Define VYSE.
content.answer: Single Engine Best Rate of Climb Speed (blue radial) — the speed at which the best rate of climb is achieved with the critical engine inoperative, live engine at take-off power, and gear retracted.
distractors: Minimum Control Speed in the air — the minimum speed at which directional control can be maintained with the critical engine inoperative, live engine at take-off power, gear retracted.
Single Engine Safety Speed — the speed below which intentional simulated engine failures should not be conducted, providing a margin above VMCA.
Minimum Control Speed on the ground — the minimum speed on the ground at which directional control can be maintained using only aerodynamic controls with the critical engine inoperative.

content.question: After a comms failure in controlled airspace, what squawk code should be set?
After comms failure in IMC, if no clearance limit has been received and acknowledged, what should you do?
content.answer: Proceed in accordance with the latest ATC route clearance acknowledged and climb to planned level.

content.question: After comms failure in IMC with a clearance limit involving an altitude or route restriction received and acknowledged, what must you do first?
content.answer: Maintain last assigned level (or MSA if higher) for 3 minutes and/or hold at the nominated location for 3 minutes, then proceed in accordance with the latest ATC route clearance acknowledged and climb to planned level.
distractors: Immediately proceed in accordance with the latest ATC route clearance acknowledged and climb to planned level.
Maintain last assigned level (or MSA if higher) for 2 minutes and/or hold for 2 minutes, then proceed in accordance with the latest ATC route clearance.
Climb to MSA/LSALT immediately, then maintain last assigned vector for 2 minutes before proceeding.

content.question: After comms failure while receiving an ATS surveillance service, what actions are required?
content.answer: Climb to MSA/LSALT; if being vectored, maintain last assigned vector for 2 minutes, then proceed in accordance with latest ATC clearance acknowledged.
distractors: Maintain last assigned level for 3 minutes, hold at the nominated location for 3 minutes, then proceed in accordance with latest ATC clearance.
Climb to MSA/LSALT; if being vectored, maintain last assigned vector for 3 minutes, then proceed in accordance with latest ATC clearance.
Squawk 7600, maintain last assigned heading and altitude for 5 minutes, then proceed to destination.

content.question: After comms failure while holding, what must you do before proceeding?
content.answer: Fly one more complete holding pattern, then proceed in accordance with latest ATC route clearance acknowledged.
distractors: Exit the hold immediately and proceed in accordance with the latest ATC route clearance acknowledged.
Fly two more complete holding patterns, then proceed in accordance with latest ATC route clearance.
Hold for 3 minutes, then proceed in accordance with the latest ATC route clearance acknowledged.

content.question: After comms failure, if visual at minima at a controlled aerodrome, when may you land?
content.answer: When a clearance to land is received via a voice modulated NAVAID and/or light signal from the Tower.

content.question: How does a pilot in flight acknowledge a light signal during the day?
content.answer: By rocking the aircraft's wings (not expected on base and final legs of the approach).

content.question: How does a pilot in flight acknowledge a light signal at night?
content.answer: By flashing on and off twice the aircraft's landing lights, or if not equipped, by switching on and off twice its navigation lights.

content.question: After comms failure in VMC in Class G airspace, what are the required actions?
content.answer: Remain in VMC; broadcast intentions prefixed with 'TRANSMITTING BLIND'; remain VFR in Class G airspace and land at the nearest suitable aerodrome; report arrival to ATS if on SARTIME or reporting schedules.

modules.title: VMC, Transponder Codes & Light Signals
modules.summary: VMC minima by airspace class, what each transponder code means, and light signals you might actually see.

content: AIP ENR 1.6 Para 7.1.4 — Class G VMC: 5,000 m visibility below 10,000 ft; 1,500 m horizontal and 1,000 ft vertical cloud clearance.
AIP ENR 1.6 — Class D VMC: 600 m horizontal and 1,000 ft vertical cloud clearance, 500 ft below cloud.
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 12 — Transponder codes: VFR OCTA 1200; IFR OCTA 2000; IFR Class E or civil CTA 3000.
1200 — transponder code for VFR in Class E, G, or OCTA
2000 — transponder code for IFR OCTA
3000 — transponder code for IFR in Class E or civil flights in CTA
5000 m — horizontal visibility for VMC in Class G below 10,000 ft
1500 m horizontal / 1000 ft vertical — cloud clearance for Class G VMC
Using squawk 1200 for IFR OCTA — code 2000 is required for IFR OCTA; 1200 is for VFR only.
Confusing Class D and Class G cloud clearance requirements — Class D requires 600m/1000ft/500ft below; Class G requires 1500m/1000ft (no below-cloud requirement at most altitudes).
Forgetting the 'clear of cloud and surface in sight' provision in Class G below 3000ft AMSL or 1000ft AGL — VMC below these heights may substitute normal minima if radio is carried and used.

content.question: What is the horizontal visibility requirement for VMC in Class G airspace below 10,000 ft?
content.answer: 5,000 m (8 km above 10,000 ft).

content.question: What are the cloud clearance requirements for VMC in Class G airspace?
content.answer: 1,500 m horizontally and 1,000 ft vertically. Below 3,000 ft AMSL or 1,000 ft AGL (whichever is higher), an aircraft may remain clear of cloud and in sight of ground or water if radio is carried and used on appropriate frequency.

content.question: What are the VMC cloud clearance requirements for Class D airspace?
content.answer: 600 m horizontally and 1,000 ft vertically, with 500 ft below cloud.

content.question: What transponder code is used for VFR in Class E, G, or OCTA?
What transponder code is used for IFR OCTA?
What transponder code is used for IFR in Class E or civil flights in CTA?
What is the formula for VHF theoretical maximum range?
content.answer: d = 1.23 (√Htx + √Hrx), where d = distance in NM, Htx = transmitter elevation in feet, Hrx = receiver height above ground level in feet.

content.question: What does a steady green light signal mean to an aircraft in flight?
content.answer: Authorised to land, if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.
distractors: Return for landing — discontinue approach and reposition.
Authorised to take-off, if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.
Continue approach — runway is clear.

content.question: What does a steady red light signal mean to an aircraft in flight?
content.answer: Give way to other aircraft and continue circling.
distractors: Airport unsafe — do not land.
Return for landing.
Discontinue approach and execute missed approach.

content.question: What does a green flashing light signal mean to an aircraft in flight?
content.answer: Return for landing.
distractors: Authorised to land, if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.
Airport unsafe — do not land.
Continue approach — runway is clear.

content.question: What does a red flashing light signal mean to an aircraft in flight?
content.answer: Airport unsafe — do not land.
distractors: Give way to other aircraft and continue circling.
Return for landing.
Discontinue approach immediately and divert.

content.question: What does a steady green light signal mean to an aircraft on the ground?
content.answer: Authorised to take-off, if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.
distractors: Authorised to taxi, if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.
Authorised to land, if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.
Return to starting point on the airport.

content.question: What does a steady red light signal mean to an aircraft on the ground?
content.answer: Stop.
distractors: Taxi clear of landing area in use.
Return to starting point on the airport.
Hold position — do not taxi.

content.question: What does a red flashing light signal mean to an aircraft on the ground?
content.answer: Taxi clear of landing area in use.
distractors: Stop.
Return to starting point on the airport.
Hold position — do not taxi onto the runway.

content.question: What does a white flashing light signal mean to an aircraft on the ground?
content.answer: Return to starting point on the airport.
distractors: Taxi clear of landing area in use.
Stop.
Authorised to taxi, if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.

modules.title: Fuel & Alternate Requirements
modules.summary: How much fixed reserve do you need? When must you plan an alternate? The fuel numbers that come up in every IPC.

content: CASR 91.455 — IFR final reserve: ≤5700kg piston/turboprop 45 min; turbojet or turboprop >5700kg 30+5%; piston >5700kg 45+5%; VFR ≤5700kg 30 min.
Part 91 MOS 8.04 — If thunderstorms forecast at destination with INTER endorsement: carry additional fuel to hold 30 min or proceed to alternate.
Part 91 MOS 8.05 / AIP ENR 1.1 Para 10.7 — PAL aerodrome alternate: aircraft must have dual VHF, or single VHF + HF + 30 min holding fuel.
45 minutes — IFR final reserve for aeroplanes ≤5700 kg
30 minutes — IFR final reserve for turbojet/turboprop >5700 kg (plus 5% contingency)
45 minutes + 5% — IFR final reserve for piston >5700 kg
30 minutes — additional holding fuel for INTER thunderstorm at destination
60 minutes — additional holding fuel for TEMPO thunderstorm at destination
Applying the 30+5% rule to piston aircraft over 5700kg — piston >5700kg needs 45+5%, NOT 30+5% (that rule is for turbojet/turboprop).
Forgetting the 5% contingency fuel for aircraft >5700kg — the final reserve is 30 or 45 min PLUS 5% contingency, not just the time reserve alone.
Confusing INTER and TEMPO additional hold fuel — INTER requires 30 min additional hold; TEMPO requires 60 min additional hold.

content.question: What is the final reserve fuel requirement for an IFR flight in an aeroplane with MTOW ≤5,700 kg?
content.answer: 45 minutes.
distractors: 30 minutes.
60 minutes.
45 minutes plus 5% contingency fuel.

content.question: What is the final reserve fuel requirement for a turbojet or turboprop aeroplane over 5,700 kg on an IFR flight?
content.answer: 30 minutes, plus 5% contingency fuel.
distractors: 45 minutes, plus 5% contingency fuel.
30 minutes — no contingency fuel is required for turbojet aircraft.
45 minutes — no contingency fuel is required.

content.question: What is the final reserve fuel requirement for a piston aeroplane over 5,700 kg on an IFR flight?
content.answer: 45 minutes, plus 5% contingency fuel.
distractors: 30 minutes, plus 5% contingency fuel.
45 minutes — no contingency fuel is required for piston aircraft.
60 minutes, plus 5% contingency fuel.

content.question: Under CASR 91.455, what is the final reserve fuel for a Day VFR flight in an aeroplane with MTOW ≤5,700 kg?
content.answer: 30 minutes.
distractors: 45 minutes.
30 minutes plus 5% contingency fuel.
60 minutes.

content.question: When must a pilot plan a destination alternate for an IFR flight (weather conditions)?
content.answer: When arrival at destination will be during the currency of, or up to 30 minutes prior to forecast commencement of: cloud more than SCT below alternate minima; visibility less than alternate minima; visibility above minima but ≥30% probability of fog/mist/dust below minima; or crosswind/tailwind exceeding aircraft maximum.

content.question: What additional fuel must be carried if thunderstorms are forecast at the destination with an INTER endorsement?
content.answer: Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 30 minutes.
distractors: Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 60 minutes.
Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 45 minutes.
Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 15 minutes.

content.question: What additional fuel must be carried if thunderstorms are forecast at destination with a TEMPO endorsement?
content.answer: Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 60 minutes.
distractors: Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 30 minutes.
Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 45 minutes.
Sufficient additional fuel to proceed to a suitable alternate or to hold for 90 minutes.

content.question: What are the alternate minima for a daytime IFR flight to an aerodrome without an instrument approach procedure?
content.answer: Lowest safe altitude for the final route segment plus 500 ft, and a visibility of 8 km.
distractors: Lowest safe altitude for the final route segment plus 300 ft, and a visibility of 5 km.
Lowest safe altitude for the final route segment plus 1,000 ft, and a visibility of 8 km.
Minimum sector altitude plus 500 ft, and a visibility of 5 km.

content.question: Under Part 91 MOS 8.05, when must a destination alternate be planned for an IFR flight by night?
content.answer: When the planned destination is not served by any instrument approach procedure, or is served only by procedures that the pilot is unable to conduct.

content.question: If a GNSS receiver certified only to (E)TSO C-129 is being used for navigation, how must the alternate be planned?
content.answer: Navigation to the destination alternate must be planned using a navigation system other than GNSS.
distractors: A destination alternate is not required when using an approved GNSS receiver.
Navigation to the alternate may use GNSS provided RAIM is available at the alternate.
Navigation to the alternate must use a VOR; ADF alone is not sufficient.

content.question: Can an aerodrome served by PAL (Pilot Activated Lighting) be nominated as an alternate?
content.answer: Yes, provided the aircraft is equipped with dual VHF, or single VHF and HF and carries 30 minutes holding fuel to allow alerting of ground staff in the event of VHF failure.
distractors: No — an aerodrome with PAL cannot be nominated as an alternate unless a responsible person is in attendance.
Yes — a PAL aerodrome can always be nominated as an alternate with no additional requirements.
Yes, provided the aircraft carries single VHF and at least 45 minutes holding fuel.

content.question: When aerodrome lighting is required for an IFR arrival, how long before ETA must it be arranged to be operating?
content.answer: From at least 30 minutes before ETA to the time landing and taxiing has been completed.
distractors: From at least 15 minutes before ETA to the time landing and taxiing has been completed.
From at least 60 minutes before ETA to the time landing and taxiing has been completed.
From at least 10 minutes before departure to at least 30 minutes after take-off.

content.question: What is the Night VFR alternate requirement for aircraft within one hour of destination?
content.answer: Must provide a suitable alternate within one hour flight time, unless destination is served by a radio navigation aid (NDB/VOR) with appropriate equipment and competency, or an approved GNSS is fitted.

modules.title: Special Alternate Weather Minima
modules.summary: When can you use reduced alternate minima? What equipment qualifies? The special conditions that most pilots forget.

content: Part 91 MOS 8.08 — Special alternate weather minima require: duplicated LOC, duplicated GP, duplicated VOR, plus either duplicated DME, duplicated GNSS, or single DME and single GNSS.
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 6.2 — Special alternate minima are identified by a double asterisk adjacent to the ALTERNATE title on the approach chart.
Part 91 MOS 8.08 — Special alternate minima are NOT available when: no local METAR/SPECI or forecasting service, or no airport control service is provided.
2 asterisks — chart notation (double asterisk) that identifies special alternate weather minima
3 duplicated systems + DME/GNSS — equipment needed: dual LOC, dual GP, dual VOR + dual DME or dual GNSS or single DME+single GNSS
Assuming special alternate minima are always available at equipped aerodromes — they revert to standard minima when ATC service is absent or met services (METAR/SPECI/TAF) are unavailable.
Forgetting that VOR duplication is also required — duplicated ILS (LOC + GP) alone is insufficient; duplicated VOR plus DME or GNSS is also required.
Mistaking a single asterisk for the special minima notation — it requires a DOUBLE asterisk; a single asterisk typically notes a different operating restriction.

content.question: What dual ILS/VOR approach capability is required for special alternate weather minima to apply?
content.answer: Duplicated LOC, duplicated GP, duplicated VOR, plus either duplicated DME, duplicated GNSS, or single DME and single GNSS.
distractors: Duplicated LOC, duplicated GP, and duplicated VOR — DME and GNSS are not required.
Duplicated LOC and duplicated GP only — VOR and DME are not required.
Duplicated LOC, duplicated GP, duplicated VOR, and duplicated DME — GNSS alone is not sufficient.

content.question: How are special alternate weather minima identified on an instrument approach chart?
content.answer: By a double asterisk adjacent to the ALTERNATE title and a note detailing the special minima.
distractors: By a single asterisk adjacent to the ALTERNATE title.
By a shaded background to the minima title, similar to TAF QNH minima.
By the notation 'SPECIAL' adjacent to the published alternate minima.

content.question: When are special alternate weather minima NOT available, reverting to standard alternate minima?
content.answer: When local METAR/SPECI or forecasting services are not available, or when an airport control service is not provided.
distractors: When the aircraft is not equipped with dual ILS approach capability.
When the TAF QNH is being used instead of actual aerodrome QNH.
When the aerodrome is served by PAL and no responsible person is in attendance.

modules.title: Altimeter Checks
modules.summary: How far off can your altimeter be and still be legal? Where do you get an approved QNH? The pre-departure checks you need to nail.

content: AIP ENR 1.7 Para 1.1 — IFR altimeter must read aerodrome elevation to within 60 ft with accurate QNH set.
AIP ENR 1.7 Para 1.2–1.3 — Error >75 ft: altimeter unserviceable. Error 60–75 ft: may fly to first landing for recheck; if error still >60 ft on recheck, unserviceable.
AIP ENR 1.7 Para 1.4 — Approved QNH sources for altimeter check: AAIS, ATC, ATIS, AWIS, CA/GRS, WATIR. NOT METAR and NOT TAF.
60 ft — maximum acceptable IFR altimeter error (must read within 60 ft of aerodrome elevation)
75 ft — error at which an IFR altimeter is deemed unserviceable
60–75 ft — tolerance band: may fly to first landing for recheck; unserviceable if error persists on recheck
Using METAR QNH for the pre-flight altimeter check — METAR QNH is an observation product and is NOT an approved source for IFR altimeter checking.
Using TAF QNH for the pre-flight altimeter check — forecast QNH is not an approved source; use AAIS, ATC, ATIS, AWIS, CA/GRS, or WATIR.
Thinking 60 ft error means the altimeter is unserviceable — 60 ft is the read-within tolerance; the altimeter becomes unserviceable only at >75 ft error.
Continuing IFR after a 60–75 ft error registers again on recheck — if the error exceeds 60 ft on the SECOND check, the altimeter must be treated as unserviceable.

content.question: With an accurate QNH set, to within what error must an IFR altimeter read the nominated elevation?
content.answer: To within 60 ft.
distractors: To within 75 ft.
To within 50 ft.
To within 100 ft.

content.question: At what error must an IFR altimeter be considered unserviceable?
content.answer: If the error exceeds ±75 ft.
distractors: If the error exceeds ±60 ft.
If the error exceeds ±100 ft.
If the error exceeds ±50 ft.

content.question: If a single IFR altimeter has an error between 60 ft and 75 ft, can the flight proceed?
content.answer: Yes — flight under the IFR to the first point of landing where the accuracy of the altimeter can be re-checked is approved. If the error exceeds 60 ft on the second check, it must be considered unserviceable.
distractors: No — an error between 60 ft and 75 ft renders the altimeter immediately unserviceable for IFR flight.
Yes — flight may continue IFR for the entire flight with no restriction, as the error is within the serviceability limit.
Yes — flight may continue IFR for up to 2 hours, after which the altimeter must be re-checked.

content.question: When an aircraft has two altimeters but only requires one for the category of operation, what error makes the spare altimeter unserviceable?
content.answer: An error in excess of 75 ft — it must be placarded unserviceable and the maintenance release endorsed.
distractors: An error in excess of 60 ft — it must be placarded unserviceable and the maintenance release endorsed.
An error in excess of 100 ft — it must be placarded unserviceable and the maintenance release endorsed.
Any error — both altimeters must agree within 30 ft for IFR operations.

content.question: What are the approved sources of an accurate QNH for altimeter checking?
content.answer: AAIS, ATC, ATIS, AWIS, CA/GRS, or WATIR. QNH from an authorised weather forecast must NOT be used.
distractors: METAR, ATIS, ATC, AWIS, or AAIS.
ATC, ATIS, TAF, AAIS, or AWIS.
Bureau of Meteorology website, ATIS, ATC, or AWIS.

content.question: What error tolerance is acceptable for a VFR altimeter?
content.answer: Should read site elevation to within 100 ft (110 ft at test sites above 3,300 ft).
distractors: Should read site elevation to within 60 ft.
Should read site elevation to within 75 ft.
Should read site elevation to within 150 ft.

modules.title: Take-off Minima & Performance
modules.summary: What are the take-off minima for single vs multi-engine? When can you use lower limits? The departure rules and performance factors.

content: Part 91 MOS 15.03 — Standard IFR take-off minima: cloud ceiling 300 ft and visibility 2,000 m for non-qualifying aeroplanes.
Part 91 MOS 15.03 — Qualifying multi-engine IFR take-off minima: visibility 800 m (reducible to 550 m under specific conditions).
Part 91 MOS 15.05/15.06 — Performance factors for unfactored charts: ≤2000kg 1.15 (T/O and landing); ≥3500kg T/O 1.25; ≥4500kg landing 1.43.
300 ft — minimum cloud ceiling for standard IFR take-off (non-qualifying aeroplane)
2000 m — minimum visibility for standard IFR take-off (non-qualifying aeroplane)
800 m — minimum visibility for qualifying multi-engine aeroplane take-off
1.15 — performance factor for ≤2000 kg aircraft (take-off and landing)
1.25 — performance factor for ≥3500 kg aircraft (take-off distance)
1.43 — performance factor for ≥4500 kg aircraft (landing distance)
Applying 800m qualifying minima to a single-engine aircraft — qualifying IFR take-off minima (800m) only apply to multi-engine aeroplanes that can maintain terrain clearance after engine failure.
Forgetting to apply performance factors to unfactored charts — unfactored performance charts must have the appropriate factor (1.15/1.25/1.43) applied; failing to do so is a common IPC oral failure.
Using the wrong landing performance factor — landing distance for ≥4500kg requires a factor of 1.43, not 1.25 (which is the take-off factor for ≥3500kg).

content.question: What are the standard take-off minima for a non-qualifying aeroplane (non-low-visibility)?
content.answer: Cloud ceiling of 300 ft and visibility of 2,000 m.
distractors: Cloud ceiling of 200 ft and visibility of 1,500 m.
Cloud ceiling of 500 ft and visibility of 1,000 m.
Visibility of 800 m — no cloud ceiling requirement for IFR aircraft.

content.question: What are the standard take-off minima for a qualifying multi-engine aeroplane (non-low-visibility)?
content.answer: Visibility of 800 m (reducible to 550 m under certain conditions).
distractors: Cloud ceiling of 300 ft and visibility of 2,000 m.
Visibility of 550 m.
Visibility of 1,000 m.

content.question: What defines a qualifying multi-engine aeroplane for take-off minima purposes?
content.answer: An IFR multi-engine aeroplane operated by at least 2 pilots; or 1 pilot if piston/turboprop with operative autofeather; or 1 pilot if turbojet — and capable of maintaining terrain clearance after engine failure until reaching the minimum height for IFR flight.

content.question: Under Part 91 MOS 15.03, when must a PIC not commence take-off?
content.answer: If meteorological conditions are less than take-off minima; or if conditions for a return to land after engine failure would not be at or above landing minima for any IAP the PIC can conduct, or would not allow a visual approach.

content.question: What performance factor should be applied to take-off distance for an aeroplane with MTOW ≤2,000 kg with unfactored charts?
What performance factor should be applied to take-off distance for an aeroplane with MTOW ≥3,500 kg with unfactored charts?
What performance factor should be applied to landing distance for an aeroplane with MTOW ≤2,000 kg with unfactored charts?
What performance factor should be applied to landing distance for an aeroplane with MTOW ≥4,500 kg with unfactored charts?

modules.title: En Route — Altimetry & Navigation
modules.summary: When do you switch from QNH to standard? What's the LSALT tolerance? Altimeter settings, nav requirements and deviation rules for the cruise.

content: AIP ENR 1.7 Para 2 — Below transition: use QNH from a station within 100 NM of the route, or area QNH. Change to 1013.2 hPa after passing 10,000 ft on climb.
AIP ENR 1.1 Para 4.1 — IFR radio navigation: tracking tolerances of ±9° from the last fix must bring the aircraft within rated coverage of a radio aid within 2 hours.
AIP ENR 1.1 Para 4.6.2 — Deviations requiring immediate ATC notification: NDB ±5°, LOC/VOR half-scale deflection, DME ±2 NM, visual 1 NM.
100 NM — maximum distance from QNH station for use below the transition altitude
2 hours — maximum interval between position fixes when navigating by radio aids
±9° — maximum tracking error tolerance for the 2-hour fix interval rule
±5° — NDB tracking deviation requiring immediate ATC notification
Changing to 1013.2 hPa at or before 10,000 ft — the change must occur AFTER passing 10,000 ft, not before levelling off or at exactly 10,000 ft.
Changing to QNH after (not before) the transition layer on descent — QNH must be set BEFORE passing the transition layer, not after.
Forgetting area QNH as an alternative — if no QNH station is within 100 NM along the route, forecast area QNH is an approved alternative.

content.question: At or below the transition altitude, what altimeter setting must be used?
content.answer: Current local QNH of a station within 100 NM along the route, or the current forecast Area QNH.

content.question: Above the transition altitude, what altimeter setting must be used?
On climb, when must the altimeter setting be changed from QNH to 1013.2 hPa?
content.answer: After passing 10,000 ft and before levelling off.
distractors: Before passing 10,000 ft and prior to levelling off.
At the transition altitude, before continuing the climb.
When passing 10,000 ft exactly — it must be changed at that altitude.

content.question: On descent, when must the altimeter setting be changed from 1013.2 hPa to QNH?
content.answer: Just before passing the transition layer.
distractors: When passing the transition altitude on descent.
When passing 10,000 ft on descent.
After passing the transition layer.

content.question: What are the three approved methods of navigation under the IFR?
content.answer: (1) Approved area navigation system; (2) radio navigation system where ±9° tracking errors from last fix still bring the aircraft within rated coverage of a radio aid within 2 hours; (3) visual reference by day on route segments where VMC permits and visual position fixing requirements can be met.

content.question: What is the maximum time interval between fixes when navigating IFR by radio navigation?
content.answer: 2 hours.
distractors: 1 hour.
3 hours.
30 minutes.

content.question: What navigation deviation requires immediate ATC notification for NDB operations?
distractors: Half-scale deflection of the CDI.
±2 NM.

content.question: What navigation deviation requires immediate ATC notification for LOC or VOR?
content.answer: Half-scale deflection.
distractors: ±5° from the specified bearing.
Full-scale deflection of the CDI.
±2 NM from track.

content.question: What navigation deviation requires immediate ATC notification for DME operations?
content.answer: ±2 NM.
distractors: ±5° from the specified bearing.
±1 NM from the required arc.
Half-scale deflection of the CDI.

content.question: What navigation deviation requires immediate ATC notification for visual navigation?
content.answer: 1 NM from cleared track.
distractors: ±5° from the cleared track.
2 NM from cleared track.
Half-scale deflection of the CDI.

content.question: What navigation deviation requires immediate ATC notification for RNP/RNAV operations?
content.answer: Outside the prescribed RNP/RNAV value.
distractors: ±5° from the cleared track.
Half-scale deflection of the CDI.
±2 NM from the cleared track.

modules.title: Holding
modules.summary: Which entry do you use? What are the speed and timing limits? Holding procedures that examiners love to ask about.

content: AIP ENR 1.5 Para 3.3 — Holding speeds: ≤FL140 max 230 KIAS (or 170 KIAS Cat A/B only); FL140–FL200 max 240 KIAS; >FL200 max 265 KIAS.
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 3.3 — Outbound timing: ≤FL140 maximum 1 minute; >FL140 maximum 1.5 minutes. Timing begins abeam the fix or on attaining the outbound heading, whichever is later.
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 3.4 — All turns in holding: 25° bank or rate one, whichever requires lesser bank. Sector 1 (parallel), Sector 2 (offset/teardrop), Sector 3 (direct) entries.
230 KIAS — maximum holding speed at or below FL140
240 KIAS — maximum holding speed FL140 to FL200
265 KIAS — maximum holding speed above FL200
1 minute — maximum outbound leg time at or below FL140
1.5 minutes — maximum outbound leg time above FL140
25° — maximum bank angle for holding turns (or rate one if that requires less bank)
Starting outbound timing from overhead the fix — timing begins ABEAM the fix or on attaining the outbound heading, whichever is LATER; not from directly overhead.
Using 240 KIAS at or below FL140 — 240 KIAS applies to FL140–FL200 only; the limit at or below FL140 is 230 KIAS.
Using 1.5-minute outbound timing at or below FL140 — 1.5 minutes applies above FL140 only; at or below FL140 the maximum is 1 minute.

content.question: What is the maximum holding speed up to and including FL140?
content.answer: 230 KIAS (or 170 KIAS where the approach is limited to Category A and B only).
distractors: 240 KIAS (or 200 KIAS where the approach is limited to Category A and B only).
200 KIAS (or 170 KIAS where the approach is limited to Category A and B only).
265 KIAS.

content.question: What is the maximum holding speed above FL140 up to FL200?
content.answer: 240 KIAS.
distractors: 230 KIAS.
265 KIAS.
250 KIAS.

content.question: What is the maximum holding speed above FL200?
content.answer: 265 KIAS.
distractors: 240 KIAS.
250 KIAS.
300 KIAS.

content.question: What is the maximum outbound leg time in holding up to and including FL140?
content.answer: 1 minute, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.
distractors: 1.5 minutes, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.
2 minutes, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.
30 seconds, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.

content.question: What is the maximum outbound leg time in holding above FL140?
content.answer: 1.5 minutes, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.
distractors: 1 minute, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.
2 minutes, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.
45 seconds, or the time or distance limit specified on the chart.

content.question: What bank angle or turn rate applies to all turns in holding in nil wind?
content.answer: 25° bank angle or rate one, whichever requires the lesser bank.
distractors: Rate one — bank angle is not specified.
30° bank angle or rate one, whichever requires the lesser bank.
25° bank angle — rate one turns are not permitted in holding.

content.question: Describe the Sector 1 (Parallel) entry for a holding pattern.
content.answer: On reaching the holding fix, turn to the outbound heading for the appropriate time (from over or abeam the fix, whichever is later); then turn onto the holding side to intercept the inbound track or return to the fix; then follow the holding pattern from the second arrival.
distractors: On reaching the holding fix, turn to make good a track 30° from the reciprocal of the inbound track on the holding side; fly outbound for the appropriate time; turn to intercept the inbound track; follow the holding pattern from second arrival.
On reaching the holding fix, turn directly to follow the holding pattern. Outbound timing begins abeam the fix or from completion of the outbound turn.
On reaching the holding fix, fly the outbound track for 1.5 minutes, then turn to intercept the inbound track and follow the holding pattern.

content.question: Describe the Sector 2 (Offset/Teardrop) entry for a holding pattern.
content.answer: On reaching the holding fix, turn to make good a track 30° from the reciprocal of the inbound track on the holding side; fly outbound for the appropriate time (maximum 1 min 30 sec) or to the DME limit; turn in the direction of the holding pattern to intercept the inbound track; follow the holding pattern from second arrival.
distractors: On reaching the holding fix, turn to the outbound heading for the appropriate time; then turn onto the holding side to intercept the inbound track or return to the fix; then follow the holding pattern.
On reaching the holding fix, turn directly to follow the holding pattern. Outbound timing begins abeam the fix or from completion of the outbound turn.
On reaching the holding fix, turn to make good a track 45° from the reciprocal of the inbound track on the holding side; fly outbound for 2 minutes; turn to intercept the inbound track.

content.question: Describe the Sector 3 (Direct) entry for a holding pattern.
content.answer: On reaching the holding fix, turn directly to follow the holding pattern. Outbound timing begins abeam the fix, or from completion of the outbound turn if the abeam position cannot be determined.
distractors: On reaching the holding fix, turn to the outbound heading for the appropriate time; then turn onto the holding side to intercept the inbound track or return to the fix; then follow the holding pattern.
On reaching the holding fix, turn to make good a track 30° from the reciprocal of the inbound track on the holding side; fly outbound for the appropriate time; turn to intercept the inbound track.
On reaching the holding fix, fly straight ahead for 1 minute, then turn to intercept the inbound track and follow the holding pattern.

content.question: At what speed must jet aircraft in CTA exit an en route holding pattern?
content.answer: 250 KT IAS, unless otherwise published or advised by ATC.
distractors: 240 KT IAS, unless otherwise published or advised by ATC.
230 KT IAS, unless otherwise published or advised by ATC.
265 KT IAS, unless otherwise published or advised by ATC.

content.question: When does outbound timing begin in a holding pattern?
content.answer: Abeam the fix or on attaining the outbound heading, whichever comes later.
distractors: When overhead the holding fix.
From the start of the outbound turn.
Abeam the fix or on attaining the outbound heading, whichever comes first.

modules.title: Approach Types, QNH & AOM
modules.summary: What makes an approach 2D vs 3D? When must you have a current QNH? Approach types and aerodrome operating minima explained.

content: AIP ENR 1.5 Para 5.3 — Actual aerodrome QNH is valid for 15 minutes after receipt from an approved source (ATC/ATIS excl. TAF-only, AWIS, BOM accredited observer).
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 1.18 — Shaded minima on Airservices charts: TAF QNH applies by default; actual QNH allows a 100 ft reduction; area QNH requires 50 ft addition.
Part 91 MOS 14.03 — METAR QNH does NOT satisfy the requirement for actual aerodrome QNH prior to commencing an IAP.
15 minutes — validity of actual aerodrome QNH received from an approved source
100 ft — reduction in shaded chart minima when actual (not TAF) QNH is set
50 ft — addition to minima when area QNH is used instead of actual QNH
Using METAR QNH as the actual aerodrome QNH for an IAP — METAR is an observation product and is NOT an approved source of actual QNH for instrument approaches.
Forgetting the 15-minute clock on actual QNH — QNH obtained more than 15 minutes before the IAF must be re-obtained from an approved source.
Adding 100 ft when using actual QNH on shaded charts — actual QNH allows a REDUCTION of 100 ft below the shaded (TAF QNH) minima; the reduction is frequently applied in the wrong direction.

content.question: Name the 2D instrument approach types.
content.answer: NDB, VOR, DME or GNSS Arrival Procedure, RNP APCH LNAV, RNP APCH LP, LLZ.

content.question: Name the 3D instrument approach types.
content.answer: ILS, MLS, GLS, RNP-LNAV/VNAV (Baro), RNP-LPV.

content.question: What are the approved QNH settings prior to passing the IAF?
content.answer: Actual aerodrome QNH from an approved source; Forecast Aerodrome (TAF) QNH; or forecast area QNH.

content.question: What are the approved sources of actual aerodrome QNH for an IAP?
content.answer: ATC and ATIS (except when only TAF QNH is provided), AWIS, and Bureau of Meteorology accredited meteorological observers.
distractors: ATC, ATIS, METAR, AWIS, and Bureau of Meteorology accredited observers.
ATC, ATIS, TAF, AWIS, and Bureau of Meteorology accredited observers.
ATC, ATIS, AWIS, AAIS, and METAR.

content.question: How long is an actual aerodrome QNH valid after receipt from an approved source?
content.answer: 15 minutes.
distractors: 30 minutes from the time of receipt.
10 minutes from the time of receipt.
Until the next QNH broadcast.

content.question: Does METAR QNH meet the requirement for actual aerodrome QNH before an IAP?
content.answer: No — METAR QNH does not meet this requirement.
distractors: Yes — METAR QNH is an approved source of actual aerodrome QNH.
Yes — METAR QNH is valid provided it is less than 30 minutes old.
Only if no ATIS or ATC QNH is available.

content.question: When using forecast area QNH on an instrument approach, how must minima be adjusted?
content.answer: Increase minima by 50 ft.
distractors: Increase minima by 100 ft.
Reduce minima by 50 ft.
Increase minima by 30 ft.

content.question: On Airservices Australia charts with shaded minima backgrounds, when may minima be reduced by 100 ft?
content.answer: When an actual aerodrome QNH (not forecast) is set.
distractors: When the aerodrome forecast (TAF) QNH is set.
When the forecast area QNH is set and is within 3 hPa of the actual QNH.
Minima on shaded charts cannot be reduced — the shading indicates fixed minima.

content.question: What factors may lead an operator to set AOM higher than published landing minima?
content.answer: Type, performance and handling characteristics of the aeroplane; composition, experience and competence of the flight crew; and the means used to determine and report meteorological conditions.

modules.title: Approach Ban, Descent Below MDA/DA & Circling
modules.summary: When does the approach ban apply? What must you see to go below MDA? Circling area dimensions and the rules around going visual.

content: CASR 91.310 — Approach ban: do not descend below 1,000 ft AAL when TDZ RVR is continuously below landing minima. Applies only when ATC and RVR reports are both available.
Part 91 MOS 16 (VMC mnemonic) — Descent below MDA or continuation below DA requires: Visual reference maintained; Minima requirements adhered to; Continuously in a position to land.
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 1.6.6 — Circling is prohibited at night in 'no-circling' sectors and at all times in less than circling VMC.
1000 ft AAL — approach ban height; do not descend below this if TDZ RVR is continuously below minima
1.68 NM — circling area radius Category A (300 ft obstacle clearance)
2.66 NM — circling area radius Category B (300 ft obstacle clearance)
4.20 NM — circling area radius Category C (400 ft obstacle clearance)
5.28 NM — circling area radius Category D (400 ft obstacle clearance)
6.94 NM — circling area radius Category E (500 ft obstacle clearance)
Applying the approach ban at an aerodrome without ATC or RVR reports — the approach ban only applies where both an ATC service and RVR reporting are in operation.
Descending below MDA based on visual reference alone — all three VMC conditions must be met: visual reference AND above minima AND continuously in position to land.
Conducting a circling approach at night in a no-circling sector — circling at night in a no-circling sector is never permitted regardless of visual conditions.

content.question: Under the approach ban rule, below what height must a PIC not descend when TDZ RVR is continually below landing minima?
content.answer: 1,000 ft AAL.
distractors: 500 ft AAL.
1,500 ft AAL.
2,000 ft AAL.

content.question: Can a PIC continue below 1,000 ft AAL if the below-minima RVR is received after already passing through that height?
content.answer: Yes — the approach ban does not apply if the aircraft is already below 1,000 ft AAL when the information is received.
distractors: No — the PIC must immediately execute a missed approach regardless of when the RVR information is received.
Yes — but only if the RVR is improving and the PIC reasonably expects to be visual at the MDA.
No — the approach ban applies from the point the information is received, regardless of the aircraft's position.

content.question: The approach ban rule only applies when what two conditions exist at the aerodrome?
content.answer: An ATC service must be in operation, and RVR reports must be available for IAPs to the relevant runway.
distractors: The aerodrome must be controlled, and the weather must be below published minima.
The aerodrome must be served by an ILS, and RVR reports must be available.
An ATIS service must be in operation, and RVR reports must be available for IAPs to the relevant runway.

content.question: What does the mnemonic 'V I V A T' represent for flying below LSALT?
content.answer: Visual approach or departure; Instrument approach or departure; VMC by day; ATC clearance; Take-off or landing.

content.question: What three conditions must be met to descend below the straight-in MDA or continue below the DA (mnemonic VMC)?
content.answer: Visual reference can be maintained; Minima requirements adhered to (all elements of met minima ≥ published); Continuously in a position from which a landing can be made with normal rates of descent and manoeuvres.

content.question: What does the circling approach mnemonic 'VMC, Day Obstacle, Night MDA' mean?
content.answer: Visibility along intended flight path not less than specified for circling; Maintain visual contact with landing environment; Inside circling area; By day not below obstacle clearance; By night not below MDA until downwind, base or final.

content.question: What is the circling area radius and obstacle clearance for Category A?
content.answer: 1.68 NM radius, 300 ft obstacle clearance.
distractors: 2.66 NM radius, 300 ft obstacle clearance.
1.68 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
1.00 NM radius, 300 ft obstacle clearance.

content.question: What is the circling area radius and obstacle clearance for Category B?
content.answer: 2.66 NM radius, 300 ft obstacle clearance.
distractors: 1.68 NM radius, 300 ft obstacle clearance.
4.20 NM radius, 300 ft obstacle clearance.
2.66 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.

content.question: What is the circling area radius and obstacle clearance for Category C?
content.answer: 4.20 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
distractors: 2.66 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
5.28 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
4.20 NM radius, 300 ft obstacle clearance.

content.question: What is the circling area radius and obstacle clearance for Category D?
content.answer: 5.28 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
distractors: 4.20 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
6.94 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
5.28 NM radius, 500 ft obstacle clearance.

content.question: What is the circling area radius and obstacle clearance for Category E?
content.answer: 6.94 NM radius, 500 ft obstacle clearance.
distractors: 5.28 NM radius, 500 ft obstacle clearance.
6.94 NM radius, 400 ft obstacle clearance.
8.00 NM radius, 500 ft obstacle clearance.

content.question: When is visual circling prohibited?
content.answer: In 'no circling' sectors by day in less than VMC, and at night.

modules.title: Approach Speeds, ILS Tolerances, Visual Approaches & Missed Approach
modules.summary: What category is your aircraft? How far can the ILS deflect before you go around? Approach speeds, tolerances and missed approach triggers.

content: AIP ENR 1.5 Para 1.15 — Aircraft category speeds: Cat A VAT <91kt, Cat B 91–120kt, Cat C 121–140kt, Cat D 141–165kt, Cat E 166–210kt.
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 7.3 — ILS tolerances: maintain within half-scale LOC and GS deflection for obstacle clearance; full-scale deflection after FAP requires immediate missed approach.

AIP ENR 1.5 Para 1.9.1 — ROFL mnemonic for mandatory missed approach: Straight-in cannot be effected without circling; Not visual at DA/MDA; RAIM warning; Outside navigational tolerance on final; Failure/suspect aid below MSA; Lost visual reference during circling.
91 kt — VAT speed separating Category A (<91) and Category B (91–120)
100 kt — maximum circling speed Category A
135 kt — maximum circling speed Category B
Half-scale — LOC/GS deflection tolerance for obstacle clearance during ILS
Full-scale — ILS deflection requiring mandatory missed approach after FAP
30 NM — maximum range for discontinuing an IAP to conduct a visual approach
Continuing an ILS approach with full-scale deflection — full-scale LOC or GS deflection after the FAP requires an IMMEDIATE missed approach; obstacle clearance is no longer assured.
Exceeding category circling speed limits — Cat A circling maximum is 100kt, Cat B is 135kt; exceeding these speeds means the aircraft is outside the protected circling area.

Flying the instrument missed approach from a visual approach go-around — from a visual approach, initially climb on runway track and remain visual; the published instrument missed approach applies only when executing a missed approach from the instrument procedure.

content.question: What is the VAT speed range for a Category A aircraft?
content.answer: Less than 91 kt.
distractors: Less than 100 kt.
Less than 80 kt.

content.question: What is the VAT speed range for a Category B aircraft?
distractors: Less than 91 kt.

content.question: What is the final approach speed range for a Category A aircraft?
What is the maximum circling speed for a Category A aircraft?
distractors: 90 kt.

content.question: What is the maximum circling speed for a Category B aircraft?
What is the maximum missed approach speed for a Category A aircraft?
distractors: 90 kt.

content.question: What is the maximum missed approach speed for a Category B aircraft?
What is the maximum speed for reversal procedures for Category A aircraft?
Under ILS tolerances, what is the standard for obstacle clearance during the final approach?
content.answer: Both LOC/GLS final approach course and glideslope should be maintained within half-scale deflection.
distractors: Both LOC and glideslope should be maintained within full-scale deflection.
LOC should be maintained within half-scale deflection; glideslope within one dot.
Both LOC and glideslope should be maintained on-centre — any deflection requires a missed approach.

content.question: Under ILS tolerances, at what deflection must a missed approach be commenced?
content.answer: If at any time after the FAP the LOC/GLS final approach course or glideslope indicates full-scale deflection, a missed approach must be commenced.
distractors: If LOC or glideslope indicates half-scale deflection at any time after the FAP.
If LOC or glideslope indicates full-scale deflection before the FAP.
If glideslope indicates full-scale deflection — LOC full-scale does not require a missed approach above MDA.

content.question: By day, when may a pilot discontinue an IAP and conduct a visual approach?
content.answer: Within 30 NM, clear of cloud, in sight of ground or water, and visibility is 5,000 m or the aerodrome is in sight.

content.question: By night, when may a pilot conduct a visual approach?
content.answer: Within 30 NM, continuous visual reference to ground or water, and visibility 5,000 m. If being vectored, must be assigned MVA and given heading/tracking to intercept final or position within circling area.

content.question: What does the missed approach mnemonic 'So Not Visual ROFL' mean?
content.answer: Straight-in landing cannot be effected without circling conditions; Not visual at DA/MDA; RAIM warning or loss after IAF or when below MSA; Outside navigational tolerance during final; Failure or suspect aid below MSA; Lost visual reference during circling.

content.question: What is the navigational tolerance for NDB-based operations?
content.answer: ±5° from the specified bearing.
distractors: ±10° from the specified bearing.
Half-scale deflection of the CDI.
±2 NM from track.

content.question: What is the navigational tolerance for VOR or LOC-based operations?
content.answer: Full-scale deflection of the CDI.
distractors: Half-scale deflection of the CDI.
±5° from the specified bearing.
±2 NM from track.

content.question: What is the navigational tolerance for DME-based operations?
content.answer: ±2 NM from the required arc.
distractors: ±1 NM from the required arc.
±5° from the specified bearing.
Half-scale deflection of the CDI.

content.question: What is the navigational tolerance for visual navigation operations?
content.answer: 1 NM from the cleared track.
distractors: 2 NM from the cleared track.
±5° from the cleared track.
Half-scale deflection of the CDI.

content.question: In the event of a go around from a visual approach in VMC, what must an aircraft initially do?
content.answer: Climb on runway track, remain visual, and await ATC instructions. If unable to clear obstacles on runway track, the aircraft may turn.

content.question: When unable or unwilling to land from an instrument approach in VMC, what procedure must be flown?
content.answer: The published instrument missed approach procedure for the instrument approach being flown, unless ATC directs otherwise.

modules.title: Landing Minima
modules.summary: How do lighting and equipment change your landing minima? The adjustments for precision and non-precision approaches.

content: Part 91 MOS 15.10 — Precision approach minima: 800m when TDZ RVR unavailable or ALS >420m inoperative; 1200m when no FD/HUD/AP, no HIRL, or ALS >210m inoperative; 1500m when entire ALS inoperative.
Part 91 MOS 15.10 — When a controlled aerodrome lighting failure doubles runway edge light spacing (e.g., 60m to 120m): apply 1.5x the charted RVR or visibility.
Part 91 MOS 15.10 — NPA with inoperative ALS: use charted visibility plus a value equivalent to the published length of the approach lighting system.
800 m — minimum visibility when TDZ RVR unavailable or ALS inoperative beyond 420 m
1200 m — minimum visibility when no FD/HUD/AP, no HIRL, or ALS inoperative beyond 210 m
1500 m — minimum visibility when the entire approach lighting system is inoperative
1.5x — multiplier for charted minima when runway edge light spacing is doubled
420 m — ALS distance threshold triggering 800 m minimum visibility
210 m — ALS distance threshold triggering 1200 m minimum visibility
Applying precision approach minima tables to NPA — NPA has its own specific rule: charted visibility plus the published ALS length when the ALS is inoperative.
Confusing the 210m and 420m ALS thresholds — ALS inoperative beyond 420m triggers 800m; ALS inoperative beyond 210m triggers 1200m; mixing these up is a common IPC failure.

Forgetting the 1.5x rule for doubled runway edge light spacing — a controlled aerodrome lighting failure that doubles spacing (e.g., 60m→120m) requires 1.5 times the charted minima, not the standard degraded lighting rule.

content.question: For a precision approach or RNP-LPV, when must minimum visibility be at least 800 m?
content.answer: If the TDZ RVR report is not available, or the approach lighting normally available beyond 420 m from the threshold is inoperative.
distractors: If the approach lighting normally available beyond 210 m from the threshold is inoperative.
If the approach lighting system normally available for the runway is entirely inoperative.
If the approach cannot be flown to landing minima using a flight director, HUD or autopilot.

content.question: For a precision approach, when must minimum visibility be at least 1,200 m?
content.answer: If the approach cannot be flown to landing minima using a flight director, HUD or autopilot; or the aircraft lacks an operative failure warning system for primary AHRS; or HIRL is not in operation; or approach lighting normally available beyond 210 m from threshold is inoperative.
distractors: If the TDZ RVR report is not available, or approach lighting normally available beyond 420 m from the threshold is inoperative.
If the approach lighting system normally available for the runway is entirely inoperative.
If the aircraft is not equipped with an ILS or autopilot capable of flying to minima.

content.question: For a precision approach, when must minimum visibility be at least 1,500 m?
content.answer: If the approach lighting system normally available for the runway is entirely inoperative.
distractors: If the TDZ RVR report is not available.
If approach lighting normally available beyond 210 m from the threshold is inoperative.
If the aircraft does not have a serviceable autopilot or flight director.

content.question: For a precision approach, when must minimum visibility be 1.5 times the charted RVR or visibility?
content.answer: If a lighting failure at a controlled aerodrome results in doubled spacing of runway edge lights (e.g., from 60 m to 120 m spacing).
distractors: If the approach lighting system normally available for the runway is entirely inoperative.
If the TDZ RVR report is not available.
If the approach cannot be flown using a flight director or autopilot.

content.question: For an NPA (RNP APCH-LNAV or similar), if the approach lighting system is inoperative, how is minimum visibility adjusted?
content.answer: The charted visibility plus a value equivalent to the published length of the approach lighting system.
distractors: The charted visibility plus 500 m.
Increase to 1,500 m — same as a precision approach.
The charted visibility plus 1,000 m.

content.question: During a circling manoeuvre, which minima override the NPA approach minima?
content.answer: The circling minimum altitude and visibility specified on the instrument approach chart, or AFM/operations manual minima — whichever is highest.

modules.title: Performance Limitations
modules.summary: What factors should you apply to unfactored AFM charts? Who's responsible for runway length — you or ATC? Performance planning essentials.

content: CASR 91.795 / Part 91 MOS 24.02 — For small aeroplanes with unfactored take-off performance charts, the pilot is responsible for ensuring the runway is long enough to take off safely (CASR 91.410).
CASR 91.800 / Part 91 MOS 25.02 — For small aeroplanes with unfactored landing performance charts, the pilot is responsible for ensuring the runway is long enough to land safely.
Part 91 Plain English Guide — Recommended factors for unfactored charts account for pilot competency variation and aircraft performance degradation with age.
1.15 — take-off factor for MTOW ≤2,000 kg (unfactored charts)
1.25 — take-off factor for MTOW ≥3,500 kg (unfactored charts)
1.15 — landing factor for MTOW ≤2,000 kg (unfactored charts)
1.43 — landing factor for MTOW ≥4,500 kg (unfactored charts)
Linear interpolation — used between the weight breakpoints for both take-off and landing
Applying the take-off factor to landing distance (or vice versa) — the landing factor for heavy aircraft is 1.43, significantly higher than the take-off factor of 1.25.
Forgetting the factors apply only to aeroplanes with unfactored charts — transport category aircraft with factored performance data use their own factored figures.
Using the wrong weight breakpoint — the take-off cutoff is at 2,000/3,500 kg, the landing cutoff is at 2,000/4,500 kg. The upper thresholds differ.
Assuming the AFM covers all conditions — slope, contamination, wind shear and temperature extremes may not be included in older or simple performance charts.

content.question: What performance factor is recommended for take-off in an aeroplane with MTOW ≤2,000 kg and unfactored charts?
What performance factor is recommended for take-off in an aeroplane with MTOW ≥3,500 kg and unfactored charts?
What performance factor is recommended for landing in an aeroplane with MTOW ≥4,500 kg and unfactored charts?
Under CASR 91.410, who is responsible for ensuring the runway is long enough for take-off and landing?
content.answer: The pilot in command.
distractors: The operator.
CASA, via the aircraft's AFM certification.
The aerodrome operator.

modules.title: VHF Range & Radar/ADS-B Coverage
modules.summary: How do you calculate VHF range? Where does radar actually cover in Australia? The coverage gaps worth knowing about.

content: VHF range formula — d = 1.23 (√Htx + √Hrx), where d = distance in NM, Htx = transmitter elevation (ft), Hrx = receiver height AGL (ft).
ADS-B coverage — Airservices Australia publishes coverage charts. At 5,000 ft AGL, coverage is available over most populated areas but significant gaps exist in remote and low-altitude operations.
Below ADS-B/SSR coverage — IFR OCTA procedures apply; squawk 2000; position reports via HF or SATCOM where available.
d = 1.23 (√Htx + √Hrx) — VHF theoretical maximum range formula in NM
5,000 ft AGL — approximate floor for reliable ADS-B coverage in most areas
Confusing Htx (transmitter elevation AMSL) with Hrx (receiver height AGL) — they use different references in the formula.
Assuming ADS-B coverage equals radar coverage — ADS-B requires aircraft equipment; radar is ground-independent.
Operating in Class G below ADS-B coverage and forgetting position reporting obligations — IFR OCTA still requires regular position reports.

content.question: What is the formula for VHF theoretical maximum range?
content.answer: d = 1.23 (√Htx + √Hrx), where d is in NM, Htx is transmitter elevation in feet, and Hrx is receiver height AGL in feet.
distractors: d = 1.23 (Htx + Hrx), where d is in NM, Htx and Hrx are in feet.
d = 1.23 × √(Htx × Hrx), where d is in NM.
d = √Htx + √Hrx, multiplied by 1.23 nautical miles per foot.

content.question: In the VHF range formula, what does Hrx represent?
content.answer: The height of the receiver above ground level in feet.
distractors: The elevation of the receiver above mean sea level in feet.
The elevation of the transmitter above mean sea level in feet.
The height of the transmitter above ground level in feet.

modules.title: Phraseology
modules.summary: What's the correct call in Class G vs Class D vs Class C? Taxi, departure, airborne and inbound phraseology you should know by heart.

content: AIP GEN 3.4 Para 6 — Prescribed phraseologies for IFR operations in Class G, D and C airspace. Pilots must use standard phraseology.
Class G taxi call includes: callsign, aircraft type, POB (for IFR flights other than air transport operations), flight rules, departure aerodrome, destination, runway.
Class D/C taxi call includes: callsign, POB, ATIS information received, flight rules, request taxi.

Class C airborne report includes: callsign, assigned heading or track, passing altitude, climbing to level. Content varies depending on whether a heading was assigned or a procedural SID is being flown.
POB — Persons On Board: required in Class G taxi calls and Class D/C taxi calls for IFR flights other than air transport operations
SAR 1800 815 257 — SAR phone number for cancelling SARWATCH after landing at Class G aerodrome
Omitting POB from the Class G taxi call — required for IFR flights other than air transport operations.
Using a departure report format in ADS-B coverage when radio contact was not established on the ground — a different format is required, stating 'Airborne [aerodrome] [time]' to initiate SARWATCH.
Forgetting to cancel SARWATCH after landing at an uncontrolled aerodrome — call the FISO, ATC, or phone SAR 1800 815 257.
Confusing Class D and Class C airborne report formats — Class C requires heading/turning info when assigned; Class D reports are simpler departure reports.

content.question: In a Class G IFR taxi call, what information must be included that is not required in a Class D taxi call?
content.answer: Aircraft type, POB (persons on board), departure aerodrome, destination and runway.
distractors: ATIS information and POB.
Fuel endurance and POB.
Aircraft type and ATIS information.

content.question: What is the format of a Class G IFR departure report when inside ADS-B or SSR coverage?
content.answer: Callsign, position (e.g. '3 miles east of Longreach'), passing level, climbing to level, estimating next fix at time.
distractors: Callsign, departed aerodrome and time, tracking, climbing to level, estimating next fix.
Callsign, airborne time, POB, tracking, climbing to level.
Callsign, position, ATIS, passing level, climbing to level.

content.question: What is the format of a Class G IFR departure report when outside ADS-B or SSR coverage?
content.answer: Callsign, departed [aerodrome] [time], tracking [to intercept] [track], climbing to level, estimating next fix at time.
distractors: Callsign, position (NM/direction), passing level, climbing to level, estimating next fix.
Callsign, airborne [aerodrome] [time], tracking, climbing to level, estimating next fix.
Callsign, POB, departed aerodrome and time, tracking, climbing to level.

content.question: When radio contact cannot be established on the ground at a Class G aerodrome and a SARWATCH needs to begin, what format is used for the departure report?
content.answer: Callsign, airborne [aerodrome] [time], tracking [to intercept] [track], climbing to level, estimating next fix at time.
distractors: Callsign, departed [aerodrome] [time], tracking, climbing to level, estimating next fix.
Callsign, position (NM/direction), passing level, climbing to level, estimating next fix.
Callsign, POB, SARWATCH request, aerodrome, destination, climbing to level.

content.question: How do you cancel a SARWATCH after landing at a Class G aerodrome?
content.answer: Call the FISO, ATC, or phone SAR on 1800 815 257.
distractors: Broadcast 'Cancel SARWATCH' on area frequency — no phone call required.
Call the nearest Class D tower on the published frequency.
File an arrival report via NAIPS within 30 minutes of landing.

content.question: In a Class D IFR taxi call, what information is included?
content.answer: Callsign, POB (for IFR flights other than air transport), ATIS information received, flight rules, request taxi.
distractors: Callsign, aircraft type, POB, departure aerodrome, destination, runway, request taxi.
Callsign, ATIS, flight rules, POB, fuel endurance, request taxi.
Callsign, destination, ATIS, request clearance.

content.question: For a Class D IFR departure via a SID, what does the departure report include?
content.answer: Callsign, tracking via [SID name], climbing to [level].
distractors: Callsign, heading, passing level, climbing to level.
Callsign, maintaining runway heading, passing level, climbing to level.
Callsign, departed aerodrome and time, tracking, climbing to level.

content.question: For a Class C IFR airborne report when a heading has been assigned, what is the format?
content.answer: Callsign, turning [left/right] [heading], passing [level], climbing to [level].
distractors: Callsign, passing [level], climbing to [level].
Callsign, maintaining runway heading, passing [level], climbing to [level].
Callsign, tracking via [SID name], climbing to [level].

content.question: For a Class C IFR airborne report when flying a procedural SID with no assigned heading, what is the format?
content.answer: Callsign, passing [level], climbing to [level].
distractors: Callsign, turning [heading], passing [level], climbing to [level].
Callsign, tracking via [SID name], passing [level], climbing to [level].
Callsign, maintaining runway heading, passing [level], climbing to [level].

content.question: What is the format for first contact with Approach at a Class C aerodrome?
content.answer: Callsign, cleared to [level], information [ATIS designator], [visual if applicable].
distractors: Callsign, information [ATIS], [distance] on [radial], descending to [level].
Callsign, passing [level], descending to [level], information [ATIS].
Callsign, ATIS [designator], request descent.
